author: @shaunpower8826
text: I used to run a waste disposal business 30 + years ago. So dealt with hundreds of T.V's. The way you broke it was the safe way? If you had struck it from the front, it would have imploded, then exploded !! Very very dangerous ? My lad's used to love to load them first at the front of the truck and throw thing's at them ? You would hear the bang a mile away !! Had to stop that? Health and safety of course. I'm sure they carried on the moment they thought I was out of ear shot ! LoL !! Keep up the good work, I may be getting on these day's, but still hate waste. Inspirational! Thank you.

author: @malcolmwright7440
text: NO, NO, NO ! I used to know a guy called Reg, who re-gunned colour picture tubes, Wellview Picture tubes, Munt Street, Bayswater. He had his children at work with him during school holidays. His kids used to smash the unusable tubes in a skip bin, he stopped his business when then his daughter at 16 years of age had both her legs amputated due to bone cancer, caused by inhaling the phosphor from old tubes ! ALSO, the guy whose job it was to replace the fluorescent tubes at Sir Charles Gairdner Hospital was instructed to smash all the old fluoros as he placed them in the skip, as people were taking them, he died within 6 MONTHS of cancer, diagnosed from inhalation of the phosphor from breaking the tubes. Breaking any tubes containing phosphor is an absolute NO NO ! !  Evidently your body can not tell the difference between calcium and phosphorus, and the phosphorus goes strait to your bones !!

author: @BoB4jjjjs
text: A CRT tube only needs a tep to let air in at the neck, you don't need to use a sledge hammer.  But for safety, you need to be at arms length, you just need to tap it and it will hiss, after it stops hissing it is safe.  Don't use a short tool to crack the neck of the tube, there is a very slight chance it will pull you hand towards the sharp glass.  You only need to tap it from a foot away and it will crack, hiss and then it is safe.  You do want to stay away from the bit at the side of the tube just in case there is high voltage stored.  It can store enough to kill you if you are unlucky.

author: @allenshepard7992
text:
Caution - Have a flat blade screw drive to discharge high voltage that may be under that Cathode ray tube (CRT) cap. Screw driver that is 10cm /5 inch with insulated handle 
 Some scrap speaker or lamp cord wire  60 cm / 2 Foot is fine. Expose 3 cm or an inch of wire. Wrap one end around the metal shaft of the screwdriver. The other end put through the metal chassis. 
Then hold only the plastic handle and work the screw driver under the cap. You may hear a spark. Just get to the metal prong holding the cap. 
If you hear nothing but have touched the metal prong, that is good. After a few hours this things discharge. 
NOTE:  this is not advice nor instruction. Use at your own risk. There could be something else going on. Do not sue me nor come back to me. Working on CRTs is a lost art now.

author: @PressToDigitate
text: Robert:  The original CRTs were built by independent Research Inventors - like Philo Farnsworth - who were not unlike yourself.  They didn't have digital oscilloscopes and software-defined signal generators to work with.  You undoubtedly have the ability to blow laboratory glassware, and a vacuum pump handy.  Have you considered reutilizing (one or more) CRTs to build a benchtop Accelerator, for Proton Beam or Ion Implantation experiments?  Personally, I find the 'Farnsworth Fusor' fascinating - and even individual High School and College students have built them successfully.  But, for a Chemist focused on Materials Science and Electrochemistry, I would think you would find the prospects for DiY Ion Implantation, or just Surface Deposition, an irresistible temptation.  There are also some nifty analytical lab instruments that could be built from an Electron Beam assembly, co-opted from a disused CRT.   Basically, you want to open that Tube with a torch instead of a Hammer, put other things where the screen is, seal it back up with glass (you can see through), and pump it back down.  I've got a huge, heavy [working] 36" Sony Trinitron TV I've been saving to do something like this with for a long time.  If it weighed less than the QE2, I'd send it to you to play with...

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
A few comments :

The degausing coil for modern crt monitors was made from aluminium wire. Not copper. Your's is aluminium probably It was because aluminium is cheaper and the coils were only powered for a few seconds so thermal loses did not matter much.
For what the flyback transformers are concerned, i did not know they were used in microwave ovens now. They use the same trick as for switching power supplies : using high frequency to drive the coil which makes the transformer much more effective and you can use a smaller one. This will be difficult to drive for DIY projects. Also for high power output the electronics needs to be very efficient. For that you need high current gate drivers to drive the power fets. This also means the electronics and transformers will have radiated emission as if it were the BBC and need's to be well shielded to confirm to EMC requirements. That probably shows in the oven you took apart

Also for the monitor the transformer might have a cascade within. Maybe not so visible but that used to be the case. It would multiply the transformer output voltage with a certain factor and will not work at low frequencies like 50 Hz.

I'm very curious what project's you come up with for using these transformers. As I explained, it will not be easy.

author: @deslomeslager
text: We tend to forget about zero energy. Oops, no, not literally.  I mean: with just one coil you can as well generate high voltage. But the sparks will be green in stead of yellow. It is done by shorting the coil and catching the flyback with HV diodes. It even seems you can do nifty things with this 'special' form of HV. Like light up a neon bulb under water, and also put your hand inside the bowl as well. I am sure you know these videos, and I know you know they are real. I wish more people would experiment with this kind of energy. I used this kind of 'pulsing energy' to restore (lead car) batteries for instance, it will break the 'bridges' which are between the plates. And I also once showed that this kind of voltage will light up a normal (tungsten) lamp on a closed loop wire. A shorted wire. It seems to have AC properties then?

author: @beamer.electronics
text: A high voltage capacitor needs to be handled with care: If terminals are left open for a long time period charge can build-up - it's best to simply wrap/bind a copper wire around the terminals (shorting them out) for long storage. To discharge an UNPLUGGED TV tube you need to bring a good earth wire (this is VERY important and possibly connected to your earthed central heating copper pipe), to the screen wire wrapped around the tube. Now connect another wire (professionally a resistor is placed in series with this wire), from that screen wire to the metal end of a well-insulated screwdriver then slowly sneak the metal end under the HV rubber/plastic Anode cap until you hear a crack (or none if there's no charge). Warning: A sudden pulse of high voltage to somebody that has a known or perhaps unknown weak heart can kill! Not worth the risk - play safe.

replies.author: @erlendse
replies.text:
You only need to connect the tube inside(the connector) to the tube outside(woven wire).
Earth have no special significance in it.

Avoid connecting yourself to the high voltage connection directly before the discharge, since the chassis will be high voltage relative to you if you do.

replies.author: @beamer.electronics
replies.text: @erlendse Having worked discharging/handling many HV capacitors and tubes for many years, I can painfully assure you that electrons only have a very strong desire to return to earth. They don't care if it's via a good earth connection or jumping across the screwdriver and through you. If you just attempt to short-out the tube (no proper earth connection protection), the "woven wire" will then (for a moment), lift to 25KV whilst it finds a route to earth (probably via you)! Interesting to note: If a good earth connection is say, 0.1 Ohms then for a fraction of a second the current will be about 25KV/0.1 = 250 thousand Amps! Which is the reason why a resistor (in series), is used. Through the human body say, 1K to about 1M Ohm this could be a heart-stopping 25Amps down to 25mA for a longer length of time! I recommend a safety earth guard be used at all times - I lived to tell this comment.

replies.author: @beamer.electronics
replies.text: Desmond Bagley Well mentioned, bounce back - very true. Possibly due to areas within a capacitor not immediately joining-in (perhaps impeeded from), the discharge? Like a water reservoir being totally and immediately discharged, but local groundwater (in a short time), partially filling it up again. This was one of the reasons why I would wire wrap HV cap terminals. And I also agree with you that not all is known. I've always found it interesting that a plate cap can be used as an aerial. My days of writing a thesis though are a tad behind me :) Any budding EEs willing to take on the challenge?

replies.author: @erlendse
@beamer.electronics
replies.text: Desmond Bagley Thank you, and yes perhaps dielectric materials chemically conditioned/phased so that groundwater is encouraged to flow with the immediate bulk reservoir discharge, might be an advancement? What's needed is an excellent chemist - Rob are you busy? "yes very, why what do you want?" :) Joking aside and perhaps a tad left field, I've also wondered if quantum probabilities and wave functions are in play with capacitors - electrons/charge/discharge? Perhaps, the higher the voltage - the greater its effect? All the best.

The whole funnel is lead-glass, also coated with lead inside(*) and outside. So plenty lead.
The phospors, I have no idea about how dangerous it is. I just assume it's not healthy.
* I haven't found a answar to what the "red stuff" is, but it may be lead.

As for capacitors, get a rolled up foil capacitor, likely way more capacitance for the volume it takes.

And the eletron-gun is tricky to re-use, I would guess reusing it would be like start filling the whole tube with oxygen-free gas, and then working onwards from that carefully.

author: @ryanwhite9314
text:
To discharge the capacitance, you can get a screwdriver and crocodile clips, and short the metal hole bit to the band around the edge of the tube and the graphite paint that's covering most of the surface :)

Also: to get a nice implosion, you want to throw the tube about 2-3 meters, so it lands on the pointy bit. Will make a terrific boom, but, from implosion :)

I never thought that growing up around a CRT tube regunning works would have any relevance in the modern world, but there you go!

author: @GrantVisserUK
text: Hi Robert, lol that is really great news about the microwave oven flybacks. What are the performance characteristics like for the new flyback vs the old MOT? MOT's were great because of the high current they could deliver, I am guessing the new flyback is trading higher voltage for lower current?

replies.author: @erlendse
replies.text:
I would take for granted they are quite much the same power & voltage wise.
There is still a magnetron and the output power of the oven should be the same.

Still, you would need a quite powerful driver to get good output.
If you can use the board you would have more of the setup.

replies.author: @GrantVisserUK
replies.text: @erlendse Yea it could be although that secondary coil did not look heavy enough to support high current output. I was wondering if they had maybe also designed a new magnetron which worked at higher voltage and frequency and thus would require less current. Either way, I think this new flyback would be very well worth bench testing :-)

replies.author: @erlendse
replies.text:
@GrantVisserUK 500 mA after a doubler/tripler? Not really a lot of current.

Anything above that is overload, for common oven transformers!

There is also a filament supply (single turn, 10A?).

The magnetron is a occilator that give out 2.4xx GHz, that it's modulated by 50/60 Hz or some KHz doesn't matter a lot.

The iron blocks from old ovens are cost-cut design. Some extra primary winding turns should hopefully lower the idle current a lot.

author: @stevetobias4890
text: I have been looking for a flyback transformer for ages. I replaced my microwave oven recently only to find it has a very thin transformer about a centimetre wide on each side. Also it sits vertically instead of the standard horizontal windings of a proper high voltage transformer. Then I hear from you that it's a flyback transformer, glad I saved it. Great video Rob, thank you yet again.

replies.author: @erlendse
replies.text:
Not really "flyback", but totally a high voltage transformers.

You can also get transformers from the backligt of old flat-screen tv/display.
(CCFL/tube drivers)
